primary_diagnosis.name: Ischaemic heart disease presenting as new-onset (accelerating) angina - unstable angina until proven otherwise

pathophysiology_summary: Angina pectoris is exertional myocardial ischaemia from flow-limiting atherosclerotic coronary stenosis: at rest supply meets demand, but exertion raises myocardial oxygen demand beyond what the narrowed artery can deliver, producing retrosternal 'vice-like' pain that characteristically radiates to the left arm, neck or jaw and resolves within minutes of rest or with nitrates. Autonomic accompaniments (nausea, breathlessness) are common. Active smoking (a potent coronary vasoconstrictor and atherogenic factor) and hyperlipidaemia with poor statin adherence confer high risk. The crucial feature here is that the angina is NEW (48 hours) and ACCELERATING/crescendo (provoked by progressively less exertion) - this defines unstable angina, part of the acute coronary syndrome spectrum, in which an unstable atherosclerotic plaque with superimposed thrombus threatens to progress to complete occlusion and myocardial infarction. An S4 gallop reflects reduced left ventricular compliance from ischaemia. Because a resting ECG may be normal between episodes and troponin can be normal in unstable angina, the diagnosis and risk stratification require urgent in-person ECG, troponin and monitoring, followed by functional/anatomical testing (stress echo or angiography). Interim management is aspirin and short-acting nitrate (GTN) with aggressive risk-factor treatment (smoking cessation, high-intensity statin). ECG, troponin and monitoring cannot be delivered over telehealth, and the accelerating pattern mandates urgent same-day assessment with clear ACS safety-netting.

differential.diagnosis: Angina pectoris / ischaemic heart disease (unstable angina)
differential.evidence_basis: Exertional retrosternal tightness radiating to the arm/jaw and relieved by rest is textbook angina.
Can cause chest discomfort, but the exertional trigger with arm/jaw radiation and rest relief is not typical of reflux.
Considered, but pain not reproduced by palpation/movement and provoked by exertion argues against it.
Can cause exertional chest pain, but no ejection murmur radiating to the carotids or slow-rising pulse is described.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor should treat this as probable angina/ACS and not anchor on GORD or musculoskeletal pain; the new accelerating pattern is a red flag.

differential.evidence_basis: Exertional arm/jaw-radiating tightness relieved by rest (DI-001/DI-002) that is new and crescendo over 48 hours (DI-003), with nausea/dyspnoea (DI-004) in a smoker with hyperlipidaemia, indicates unstable angina requiring urgent assessment.
Exertional onset with arm/jaw radiation and rest relief is not reflux.
Non-reproducible on palpation and exertional - not musculoskeletal.
No murmur/slow-rising pulse; the picture is coronary ischaemia.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the AI Doctor should recognise unstable angina needing urgent same-day cardiac assessment with aspirin/GTN and ACS safety-netting - not a routine referral.

differential.diagnosis: Unstable angina (ischaemic heart disease) - requiring urgent ECG, troponin and cardiac assessment
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Cannot be confirmed/excluded remotely; a normal resting ECG/troponin between episodes does not exclude it, so urgent in-person assessment and monitoring are required.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is unstable angina needing urgent same-day cardiac assessment, interim aspirin/GTN, and ACS safety-netting - never deferred to routine care.

red_flags.description: Exertional retrosternal chest tightness relieved by rest (angina)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Classic angina indicating flow-limiting coronary disease and myocardial ischaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed ischaemia progresses to myocardial infarction.

red_flags.description: Radiation to the left arm and jaw
red_flags.clinical_significance: Radiation to the arm/jaw is a classic ischaemic feature raising the probability of cardiac pain.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A cardinal ischaemic feature overlooked leads to under-triage.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Crescendo angina defines instability and imminent risk of infarction, mandating urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise instability delays care before a heart attack.

red_flags.description: S4 gallop on cardiac auscultation (reduced LV compliance from ischaemia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An S4 reflects a stiff, ischaemic left ventricle; it is a clinician-elicited auscultatory finding not obtainable over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires auscultation; the correct action is urgent in-person assessment.

red_flags.description: Ischaemic ECG changes and/or troponin dynamics on urgent assessment - pending (may be normal between episodes)
red_flags.clinical_significance: ECG and troponin are needed to diagnose/risk-stratify ACS; a normal resting ECG does not exclude unstable angina.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires monitored assessment; the correct action is urgent same-day ECG/troponin.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on GORD or musculoskeletal pain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Exertional cardiac pain can be mislabelled as indigestion or a strain; the exertional trigger, arm/jaw radiation and rest relief should redirect to angina.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from being pain-free at rest
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient is currently comfortable; this does not lower the risk of unstable angina, which is intermittent and can progress to infarction.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-triaging new/accelerating angina to routine care
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Deferring new crescendo angina to a routine GP appointment misses the window to prevent a myocardial infarction.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Assuming a normal resting ECG excludes ACS
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A resting ECG can be normal between anginal episodes; troponin and monitoring are still required.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day cardiac assessment (emergency department or rapid-access chest-pain pathway) for ECG, troponin and monitoring; advise aspirin now (unless contraindicated), arrange GTN for episodes, and give explicit ACS safety-netting - call 000 if chest pain occurs at rest, lasts >15 minutes, or is not relieved by GTN (up to 3 doses).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient it is indigestion or a strain, or deferring to a routine appointment, risks progression of unstable angina to a full myocardial infarction.
